Executive Summary

Executive Summary

This is a 5-day-out flight with very limited forecast maturity — the synoptic picture for Monday March 30 is still evolving and the details that matter (winds aloft, icing, ceilings) simply aren't pinned down yet. Primary Risk: an approaching Pacific trough/low system that, depending on timing, could bring IMC, icing, and strong southwesterly winds over the Cascades and Sierra Nevada right when you're trying to cross at 12,000 ft. Provisional call is MARGINAL GO — the extended progs suggest the worst of the Thursday/Friday system will have exited the East Coast by Monday, but a follow-on shortwave signal shows up over the Pacific Northwest on the Day 6–7 charts, and its timing relative to your flight is genuinely uncertain at this range. The single most important variable to watch: whether the next Pacific system arrives over Oregon before or after your Monday afternoon departure.

Operational Briefing

The Day Before — What to Watch

Sunday evening March 29 is your critical go/no-go checkpoint. By then TAFs will be valid, winds aloft forecasts will be on board, and you'll have 12–24 hour model agreement to work with. Here's what to look for:

  • Check the surface prog and 500mb analysis Sunday night. Is the Pacific shortwave visible on the Day 6–7 extended charts still offshore, or has it accelerated? If it's already on the Oregon coast by Sunday night, your Monday departure gets complicated fast.
  • Pull the KBDN TAF and area forecast discussion (AFD) from the Medford WFO. If they're talking about increasing winds, lowering ceilings, or mountain wave over the Cascades for Monday, take that seriously — they know this terrain.
  • Check winds aloft at 12,000 ft (FD charts) for Bend and Redding-area waypoints. If you're seeing 250–270° at 35+ knots or more at cruise altitude over the Oregon Cascades, expect a rough ride and possible mountain wave. If it's 20 knots or less, the route looks much more benign.
  • Look at the AIRMETs for Sierra/Cascade turbulence and icing. If G-AIRMETs for moderate icing or mountain obscuration are already posted for the route, that's your warning shot.

If conditions look fine Sunday night, do another check Monday morning before departure. That final check matters most.

Leaving KBDN — Departure at 2026-03-30 21:30Z

TAFs for KBDN are not yet valid at this range — no specific ceiling or wind data is available. What the extended progs show for Monday March 30 is broadly encouraging: the Day 4 chart (valid Sunday March 29) shows a building Pacific high and relatively benign conditions over the West Coast, and the Day 5 chart (valid Monday March 30 12Z) suggests a pattern with lows back out over the Pacific and a generally westerly flow. However, by Day 6–7 (Tuesday), a new trough axis is digging into the Northwest, which raises the question of whether its leading edge reaches central Oregon by your Monday 21:30Z (1:30 PM Pacific) departure.

If the system stays offshore through Monday, KBDN departure conditions should be mostly VMC. Bend sits at 3,460 ft MSL on the east side of the Cascades — if any precip is west of the crest, you'll likely be in clear, drier air on departure. Climbout eastbound-to-south won't be terrain-challenged. The main concern on departure is whether southwesterly prefrontal flow is already kicking up over the Cascades — if so, expect some light-to-moderate chop in the climb through 8,000–11,000 ft as you transition from the east Cascades environment into the cross-mountain flow.

If the system is closer, you could be looking at increasing clouds, a possible broken layer, and stronger headwinds. That's the uncertainty to resolve by Sunday night.

The Ride at 12,000 ft

Icing

No icing chart data is available at this time range (5+ days out). No FIP, no CIP, no GTG turbulence data exists for March 30. Icing assessment is therefore based on the synoptic pattern only, and confidence is LOW.

  • If the Pacific system is still offshore, March 30 at 12,000 ft over the Cascades and northern Sierra could be mostly dry — no widespread moisture signal, limited icing risk. CONDITIONAL / LOW CONFIDENCE.
  • If the trough is arriving, the 10,000–14,000 ft band in cloud is exactly where you'd expect mixed icing (rime and possibly clear ice) in any precip or embedded cloud layers. That's your altitude range, and March conditions in the Cascades can put significant icing in that band.
  • No SLD signal present — data not available to confirm or deny. Do not plan to fly into known icing unless your aircraft is certified for it.
AltitudeIcing Risk (if system arrives)Notes
Below 8,000 ftLow–ModerateLikely above freezing if system is weak; watch for valley cloud
8,000–12,000 ftModerate (CONDITIONAL)Worst band if moisture present — your cruise altitude
12,000–14,000 ftModerate (CONDITIONAL)Could be above cloud tops if system is weak
Above 14,000 ftLowLikely above icing if cloud tops moderate; oxygen required

This table is conditional on moisture being present. If Monday is dry and clear, icing is not a factor at any altitude on this route.

Turbulence and Winds

Winds aloft data for March 30 is not yet available. Based on the Day 5 extended prog, the pattern suggests a westerly to southwesterly flow regime over the Pacific Northwest, consistent with a developing trough to the northwest. If upper winds at 12,000 ft are running 25–35 knots from the southwest, expect the following:

  • Oregon Cascades crossing (KBDN departure and southbound): 25–35 kt flow over complex terrain = light-to-moderate turbulence possible, LOCALIZED. Mountain wave can develop on the lee side even without a clear cloud signature. Why this happens: southwest flow perpendicular to the north-south Cascade crest compresses and accelerates over ridgelines, creating wave and rotor zones on the lee (east) side. Bend sits in that lee zone.
  • Northern California / Sacramento Valley: Generally smoother as terrain flattens out. Expect a better ride below 12,000 ft once clear of the Cascades.
  • Bay Area approach: If a system is nearby, expect gusty and variable surface winds at KSQL. The Bay Area coastline compresses and channels marine flow through gaps — arrival winds can be significantly different from what winds aloft suggest.

Wind → Turbulence at cruise (12,000 ft): Without actual FD data, this is conditional. If winds are under 20 kt, it'll be a smooth ride. 20–30 kt, expect light chop over the Cascades. 30+ kt, plan for light-to-moderate turbulence over ridgelines and consider whether a lower altitude (9,000–10,000 ft) keeps you below the worst wave band, or whether going higher (14,000 ft) puts you above it.

Altitude Strategy

  • Best altitude (if dry and system offshore): 12,000 ft as planned — good cruise performance, above most terrain issues, likely clear.
  • Best altitude (if system arriving): Either descend to 9,000–10,000 ft (potentially below the main icing/turbulence layer but terrain clearance matters — check MEAs) or climb to 14,000 ft (likely above cloud tops, but oxygen required and icing risk in climb). Neither is clearly "the" answer without actual icing charts.
  • Worst altitude: 10,000–12,000 ft in active cloud/precip — maximum icing exposure with no clean escape in either direction.
  • Escape option: If you pick up unexpected ice at 12,000 ft, the quickest fix is descend to warmer air. Check MEAs for the route before departure so you know your minimum safe descent altitude. Sacramento Valley floor is well below freezing level in most scenarios — descend and divert to KRDD or KSAC if needed.

Other Hazards

HazardRiskLegWhat to Expect
Mountain wave/turbulenceCONDITIONAL / LOCALIZEDKBDN departure, Cascade crossingLight-to-moderate chop if SW winds 25+ kt; smooth if winds light
Icing in cloudCONDITIONAL / LOW CONFIDENCEEntire route at 12,000 ftOnly a factor if Pacific system arrives early; no moisture signal if clear
Bay Area coastal windsCONDITIONAL / LOCALIZEDArrival KSQLSurface winds terrain-modified; could differ significantly from winds aloft

Getting Into KSQL

Estimated block time: KBDN to KSQL is roughly 230–250 nm direct. At 12,000 ft with a neutral wind, figure about 1.5–2 hours depending on your aircraft. If you've got a 25 kt headwind component, add 20–30 minutes. Estimated arrival around 23:30–00:00Z Monday/Tuesday (3:30–4:00 PM Pacific).

KSQL arrival — no TAF valid yet. San Carlos sits on the west side of the Bay, about 3 miles from the water. It's a Class E field with no instrument approach (it's a VFR-only airport). This is operationally significant: KSQL is VFR-only. You must have visual conditions to land there.

  • If Monday afternoon Bay Area conditions are VMC — which is likely if the Pacific system is still offshore — expect typical spring afternoon conditions: westerly marine flow, possibly gusty 15–20 kt winds off the Bay, good visibility.
  • If the system is arriving, the Bay Area can go IFR quickly with low stratus and marine layer thickening. Ceilings at KSQL can drop below 1,000 ft with visibility under 3 miles even without active precipitation.
  • Runway at KSQL: Runway 12/30 (roughly aligned southeast/northwest). Prevailing Bay Area afternoon westerly winds favor Runway 12 (roughly into a southeast runway — partial crosswind/headwind from the west-northwest). Surface winds at KSQL are terrain- and bay-modified; do not assume winds aloft translate directly to the runway. Estimated surface wind: 10–20 kt variable, direction uncertain at this range.

Alternates: Since KSQL is VFR-only, alternates are critical. KSFO (San Francisco International, 10 nm north) or KPAO (Palo Alto, 5 nm south) are your go-to IFR-capable alternates with approaches. Have fuel to KPAO or KSFO if KSQL is below minimums.

Fly or No

MARGINAL GO — provisional at 5 days out, forecast confidence is LOW.

The synoptic setup for Monday shows the major Thursday/Friday East Coast storm well clear. A follow-on Pacific system is signaled on the Day 6–7 extended progs, but its timing is uncertain. If it arrives Monday, you're looking at possible icing, moderate turbulence over the Cascades, and potentially VMC-only KSQL going below minimums. If it stays offshore through Monday, this is a reasonable early-afternoon VFR/IFR flight with mostly VMC and manageable winds. You cannot make a final call on this flight until Sunday night at the earliest.

One hard operational constraint regardless of weather: KSQL is VFR-only. You must be able to arrive VMC. Plan alternates and carry fuel accordingly.

What changes this call

  • Pacific system arrives early — surface low on the Oregon coast by Sunday night: System timing overlap with Monday departure = NO-GO or significant route/timing change.
  • Winds aloft at 12,000 ft exceed 35 kt from the SW over the Cascades: Mountain wave risk escalates significantly; consider lower cruise altitude or delay.
  • KSQL TAF (when available) showing ceilings below 1,500 ft or visibility below 3 SM: Hard NO-GO for KSQL specifically — divert plan to KPAO or KSFO required.
  • Icing AIRMETs posted for moderate icing along route at 12,000 ft without aircraft certification: Altitude change or route deviation required; possible NO-GO.

If You Go — Do This

  1. Sunday evening (March 29) — full weather check. Pull TAFs for KBDN and KSQL/KPAO/KSFO, AFD from Medford and Bay Area WFOs, FD winds aloft for MFR/RDD/SFO at 9,000/12,000 ft, and G-AIRMETs for the route. This is your real go/no-go checkpoint.
  2. Monday morning — confirm. Fresh PIREPs for Cascades and northern California if anyone is flying. Check CIP/FIP icing charts. If SIGMET or G-AIRMET for moderate icing is posted covering your route and altitude, reconsider.
  3. Carry fuel to your alternate. KSQL is VFR-only. File IFR to KPAO or KSFO with KSQL as destination if your aircraft and certificates allow, or plan a VFR flight with an honest look at Bay Area conditions before committing. Have fuel to KPAO (+5 nm) or KSFO (+10 nm) with reserve.
  4. File IFR if instrument rated and current. Not because you expect widespread IMC, but because the routing crosses significant terrain, the arrival environment is compressed and variable, and it gives you options if conditions deteriorate en route. IFR here is about margin, not expectation of IMC.
  5. Consider departing KBDN at 19:00–20:00Z instead of 21:30Z if forecasts look borderline — arriving Bay Area before any evening marine layer enhancement is favorable.
  6. Know your MEAs. KBDN to Bay Area via V23/V105 or direct — know the minimum safe altitudes for terrain clearance south of the Cascades and over the Siskiyous/Coast Range. If you need to descend for icing, you need to know how low you can go and stay clear.

Confidence

Confidence: LOW — forecast maturity is limited at 5 days out.

The charts that actually matter for this flight (winds aloft, icing, TAFs) don't exist yet. The extended progs show a broadly reasonable pattern for Monday with a possible trough signal on Days 6–7, but extended prog accuracy at this range is low — the trough could accelerate, stall, or fizzle. The one fixed constraint is KSQL's VFR-only status, which is an operational fact regardless of forecast confidence.

Key forecast elements still to resolve:

  • Timing and strength of next Pacific trough — the central uncertainty
  • Winds aloft at 12,000 ft over the Cascades — determines turbulence and icing risk
  • Bay Area Monday afternoon ceiling/visibility — determines whether KSQL is accessible at all
  • Icing layer extent and altitude — needs FIP charts once available

Revisit Sunday night. That's when this briefing becomes worth its salt.
